paragraphs.text: “And when Abram was ninety years old and nine, the Lord appeared to Abram, and said unto him: …be thou blameless… in the covenant which I shall set with thee” (Gen 17:1–2). “And I will multiply thee exceedingly, and will make of thee nations” — that is, into many tribes; here too of course are meant the descendants of Esau, of Keturah, and of Ishmael, who became numerous peoples. “…and kings shall come out of thee,” by which were indicated kings from the tribe of Judah and Ephraim and the Idumean kings (Gen 17:6). “And this is my covenant: every man child… shall be circumcised in the flesh of your foreskin” (Gen 17:10–11).

“And God said unto Abraham… I will give thee a child of Sarah: and I will bless him, and he shall be unto nations… And Abraham fell upon his face, and laughed, and said in his mind: Shall a child be born unto him that is a hundred years old? and shall Sarah, that is ninety years old, bear? And he said: O that Ishmael might live before Thee” (Gen 17:15–18). Abraham laughed not because he doubted; and when he said: “let Ishmael live,” he only revealed his love for Ishmael. For twenty-five years God left him in hope of having offspring, and Abraham at all the revelations made to him bore witness to his faith. As much as he had struggle with barrenness, just so much did he show the triumph of faith. But when to barrenness was added old age, then Abraham laughed in his thought — that is, marveled that God would do this with him. Therefore God says to him: “Truly Sarah thy wife shall bear thee a son.” I do not say this to tempt thee, nor that thou shouldest believe what I do not fulfill. “And as for Ishmael, I have heard thee: and behold, I have blessed him… and will multiply him” (Gen 17:19–20). If Abraham had doubted, God would not have assured him by an oath of the truth of His word; would not have heeded Abraham’s prayer for Ishmael; would not have announced that in the next year a son would be born to Abraham; but would have reproved and accused Abraham. And what is said of Ishmael, that he would beget twelve princes, means that from Ishmael would come twelve tribes, as from Jacob came twelve tribes. On that day Abraham performed the circumcision upon himself, upon Ishmael his son, and upon all his household.
